<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Multiple execution log files (.jsonl and .1.jsonl) indicate log rotation is active; confirm a retention policy exists so logs do not grow unboundedly in long-running projects.
[observation] [info] Runtime state files are cleanly separated from source code, which is correct; the zone boundary acts as a natural audit surface for all PRD mutations.
[observation] [info] pending-proposals.json and acknowledged-findings.json are ephemeral workflow state; documenting their lifecycle (when they are created, consumed, and cleared) would help contributors understand the analyze→accept flow.
[suggestion] [warning] Document or enforce a two-tier retention policy within .rex/: persistent files (prd.json, config.json, archive.json) should be committed and backed up, while ephemeral files (pending-proposals.json, acknowledged-findings.json, execution-log*.jsonl) should be gitignored or pruned automatically — mixing them in one directory with no policy distinction creates operational risk if someone treats ephemeral state as authoritative.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- All files are pure data (JSON/Markdown) with no imports, giving this zone perfect cohesion and zero coupling; it functions correctly as a runtime artifact directory, not a source zone.
- The presence of both execution-log.jsonl and execution-log.1.jsonl suggests log rotation is working, but tooling should ensure old logs are pruned or archived to prevent unbounded growth.
- This zone should never contain source code; if a future contributor accidentally adds a .ts file here it will merge into this zone and distort cohesion metrics.
- Runtime state files are cleanly separated from source code, which is correct; the zone boundary acts as a natural audit surface for all PRD mutations.
- Multiple execution log files (.jsonl and .1.jsonl) indicate log rotation is active; confirm a retention policy exists so logs do not grow unboundedly in long-running projects.
- pending-proposals.json and acknowledged-findings.json are ephemeral workflow state; documenting their lifecycle (when they are created, consumed, and cleared) would help contributors understand the analyze→accept flow.
- The zone conflates two fundamentally different data lifecycles: persistent state (prd.json, config.json, archive.json) which should survive indefinitely, and ephemeral workflow state (execution-log.jsonl, pending-proposals.json, acknowledged-findings.json) which is consumed and cleared during normal operation — no tooling or documentation currently enforces different retention policies for these two classes.
- workflow.md sits at the boundary between ephemeral state and reference documentation; its format (Markdown) suggests human-readable reference, but its content tracks transient workflow position — this dual nature makes it unclear whether it should be in .rex/ or docs/.
- Document or enforce a two-tier retention policy within .rex/: persistent files (prd.json, config.json, archive.json) should be committed and backed up, while ephemeral files (pending-proposals.json, acknowledged-findings.json, execution-log*.jsonl) should be gitignored or pruned automatically — mixing them in one directory with no policy distinction creates operational risk if someone treats ephemeral state as authoritative.

</insights>
